records.ety: [OE. vernish, F. vernis, LL. vernicium; akin to F. vernir to varnish, fr. (assumed) LL. vitrinire to glaze, from LL. vitrinus glassy, fr. L. vitrum glass. See Vitreous.]

defs: A viscid liquid, consisting of a solution of resinous matter in an oil or a volatile liquid, laid on work with a brush, or otherwise. When applied the varnish soon dries, either by evaporation or chemical action, and the resinous part forms thus a smooth, hard surface, with a beautiful gloss, capable of resisting, to a greater or less degree, the influences of air and moisture.
notes: ☞ According to the sorts of solvents employed, the ordinary kinds of varnish are divided into three classes: spirit, turpentine, and oil varnishes.

defs: That which resembles varnish, either naturally or artificially; a glossy appearance.
quotes: The varnish of the holly and ivy.

defs: An artificial covering to give a fair appearance to any act or conduct; outside show; gloss.
quotes: And set a double varnish on the fame The Frenchman gave you.

records.ety: [Cf. F. vernir, vernisser. See Varnish, n.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Varnished (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Varnishing.]

defs: To lay varnish on; to cover with a liquid which produces, when dry, a hard, glossy surface; as, to varnish a table; to varnish a painting.
To cover or conceal with something that gives a fair appearance; to give a fair coloring to by words; to gloss over; to palliate; as, to varnish guilt.
quotes: Close ambition, varnished o'er with zeal.
Cato's voice was ne'er employed To clear the guilty and to varnish crimes.
